title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Ethics of Genetic Engineering in Agriculture

texts.A: The demonization of Genetically Modified Organisms (GMOs) is a luxury of the well-fed, a position that can only be held comfortably by those who have never faced genuine food insecurity. [39]In the developing world, pest-resistant and drought-tolerant crops are the difference between life and death.[/39] Smallholder farmers across sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia depend on these innovations to protect their harvests from climate volatility and devastating infestations. [40]The scientific consensus is clear: these crops are as safe as their conventionally bred counterparts.[/40] This conclusion has been confirmed by hundreds of independent studies conducted over several decades, yet public mistrust continues to undermine rational policy. By blocking the export of GMO technology, wealthy nations are effectively imposing their aesthetic and philosophical preferences on those who cannot afford such indulgences. The contradiction is profound. [38]We must prioritize global food security over irrational fears of 'Frankenfood.'[/38] Science, not sentiment, must guide our agricultural future.
texts.B: The primary danger of GMOs is not biological, but economic, and this crucial distinction is rarely made in mainstream public debate. [37]The current model allows a handful of multinational corporations to patent the very DNA of our food supply.[/37] This creates a dangerous and exploitative dependency for farmers across the developing world, who are forced to buy new seeds every year rather than saving them from their previous harvest, as agricultural communities have done for millennia. This systematic dismantling of agricultural self-sufficiency represents a profound and largely unreported transfer of power from rural communities to distant corporate boardrooms. The financial implications for smallholder farmers are severe and largely unacknowledged. [40]I share the skepticism regarding the 'safety' narrative; while the crops themselves may be harmless, the increased use of proprietary herbicides associated with them is devastating for local biodiversity and soil health.[/40] We are trading one set of problems for a far more complex and poorly understood set of others.
texts.C: Genetics is a powerful tool, but it is being used to solve the wrong problems, and we must be far more deliberate about the direction of this research. Instead of engineering crops to survive massive doses of proprietary chemicals, we should be using genetic science to enhance the nutritional profile of ancient, climate-resilient grains that have sustained human populations for thousands of years. The focus on corporate-controlled 'monocultures' that prioritize yield over biological diversity is a recipe for eventual ecological collapse. Yet a blanket political opposition to the technology itself is equally short-sighted and misguided. [39]If genetic engineering were handled by public institutions for the public good, it could be a cornerstone of a genuinely sustainable future.[/39] The problem is not the science; it is the privatization of the science. We urgently need open-source, publicly funded research programs to redirect this powerful tool toward genuine and lasting human flourishing rather than corporate profit.
texts.D: We must acknowledge that our current agricultural methods are profoundly unsustainable, but GMOs are a sticking plaster, not a cure for the underlying disease afflicting our food systems. The real solution lies in regenerative agriculture and strategic crop rotation, which restore the soil's natural vitality rather than depleting it season after season. These methods have been proven effective in diverse climates and can be adopted without the intervention of global corporations or proprietary technologies. [37]I share the view that corporate patenting of seeds is a serious threat to global food sovereignty.[/37] Small farming communities around the world are being dispossessed of traditional knowledge that has been part of their cultural heritage for generations. [38]When profit is the primary motive for genetic alteration, the health of the consumer and the environment will always be secondary to the bottom line of the shareholder.[/38] We must redesign our food systems from the ground up, not simply upgrade the existing machinery of exploitation.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar opinion to B regarding the corporate control of the food supply?
questions.explanation: Expert B is concerned about 'multinational corporations' patenting DNA. Expert D agrees, stating that 'corporate patenting of seeds is a threat to global food sovereignty.'

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the primary motivation for promoting GMO technology?
questions.explanation: Expert A believes the motivation is 'global food security.' Expert D argues that 'profit is the primary motive' and that the health of the environment is secondary.

questions.question: Which expert shares C's view on the potential for genetic engineering to be used positively?
questions.explanation: Expert C thinks it could be a 'cornerstone of a sustainable future' if handled for the public good. Expert A also sees it as a vital tool for 'life and death' situations and food security.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the safety of GMO crops themselves?
questions.explanation: Expert B is skeptical of the 'safety' narrative. Expert A states that the 'scientific consensus is clear' that they are as safe as conventional crops.
